q: Brighthelm Church and Community Centre Brighton

raw.name: Brighthelm Church and Community Centre
raw.wheelchair:description: On steep hill. Café and Conference Hall fully accessible. Double powered doors open inward from street. Accessible WC close to cafe. [Loops at cafe & in Hall] Church not checked.

raw.wheelchair:description: Steps from Grand Junction Road, boardwalk from lower promenade can get covered with pebbles.

raw.name: Fingermaze
raw.note: The artwork is based around a thumbprint representing man’s influence on the landscape. It is formed with York stone slabs on a lime mortar base.

raw.artwork_subject: Juggler on unicycle with three fire torches
raw.inscription: 'The Juggler' by Helen Collis presented by Dr Martin Hildyard husband of Helen Collis, and by Helen's family
raw.name: The Juggler

raw.inscription: Rest and be Thankful
raw.name: Boundary stone (Devil's Stone)

raw.description: Monthly curated shows in 'Brighton’s Smallest Gallery Space': a pair of old red phone boxes.
raw.name: Dog and Bone Gallery

raw.inscription:
AUBREY BEARDSLEY
Master Of Line
WAS BORN IN THIS HOUSE
ON AUGUST 21ST 1872
HE WAS A PUPIL AT THE
BRIGHTON GRAMMAR
SCHOOL 1884-1888
AND DIED AT MENTONE
ON MARCH 16TH 1898
raw.name: Aubrey Beardsley
raw.wikimedia_commons: File:Aubrey Beardsley Master of Line was born in this house plaque 2023-06-22.jpg

raw.name: Steve Ovett's Foot
raw.note: The bulk of the original statue of the famous Brighton-born runner was stolen for scrap metal, leaving only Steve's foot. A new statue now exists on Brighton seafront.
raw.wikimedia_commons: Category:Steve Ovett statue, Preston Park, Brighton

raw.name: Kissing Coppers
raw.note: This is a reproduction. The original appeared at the exact same location but was removed and sold.

raw.inscription: Thomas Read Kemp, founder of Kemp Town, lived here from 1827 to 1837
raw.name: Thomas Read Kemp

raw.name: War Memorial

raw.name: Thomas Cubitt

raw.inscription: Max Miller (1894‒1963) lived here ‘The pure gold of Music Hall’
raw.name: Max Miller

raw.inscription: Back to the Future Roger Marlowe 1813-1890 Pioneer of time travel lived here in 2099
raw.name: Roger Marlowe

raw.inscription: David Mocatta 1806-1882 Anglo-Jewish Architect Remodelled this building as the City's first purpose built Synagogue in 1838
raw.name: David Mocatta

raw.inscription: Captain Ronald Taylor 1916-2002 WW2 sabotage agent in Italy - Special Operations Executive born near this site at 29 Carlton Street
raw.name: Captain Ronald Taylor

raw.description: Can-can dancer's legs on the roof. Acquired from the closed Not The Moulin Rouge Theatre in Oxford.

raw.name: Hablot Knight Browne 'Phiz'

raw.inscription: In 1905 this house became the Lewes Road Hospital. Opened by Dr Helen Boyle it was the first in England to care for poor women suffering from nervous breakdown. In 1912 it moved to larger premises in Hove.
raw.name: Lewes Road Hospital

raw.description: Part of the art installation “A Simple Act of Wonder” (2020) by Walter Hugo and Zoniel Burton, commissioned by Fabrica & Brighton Festival.
raw.inscription: When the Bevy was first built, Moulsecoomb was an estate of Temperance, with no alcohol allowed. Originally, a gate was cunningly placed here to convince planners that the pub was in Bevendean, when actually it was in Moulsecoomb. Miraculously, it worked.
raw.name: The Bevy

raw.inscription: Lawyer Sir Edward Marshall Hall K.C. ‘The Great Defender’ born here 1858
raw.name: Sir Edward Marshall Hall

raw.inscription: Margaret Powell 1907-1984 author and broadcaster lived here
raw.name: Margaret Powell

raw.inscription: Dora Bryan OBE 1923-2014 actress and comedienne lived here 1975-2008 formerly Clarges Hotel
raw.name: Dora Bryan

raw.inscription: Richard Addinsell composer lived here from 1960 to 1977
raw.name: Richard Addinsell
